skill.name: azure-quotas
skill.description: Check and manage Azure quotas and usage across providers. For deployment planning, capacity validation, and region selection.
skill.summary: Azure quota management skill for checking service limits, current usage, and requesting quota increases via Azure CLI.
skill.author: Microsoft

security_audit.summary: All 185 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The scanned files are documentation (.md) containing Azure CLI command examples, not executable code. Shell commands in backticks are documentation syntax for illustrating CLI usage. URL references point to official Microsoft documentation. No actual code execution, network calls, or malicious patterns exist. This is a legitimate Microsoft skill for Azure quota management.

content.user_title: Manage Azure quotas and service limits
content.value_statement: Prevent deployment failures by checking Azure quota availability before provisioning resources. Get accurate capacity information across regions using Azure CLI quota commands.
actual_capabilities: List all quota limits for Azure resource providers in a region
Show current quota usage and available capacity for specific resources
Compare quota availability across multiple Azure regions
Request quota increases via Azure CLI commands
Track quota request status and operations
Discover correct quota resource names via CLI discovery
limitations: Requires Azure CLI with quota extension installed (az extension add --name quota)
Not all Azure resource providers support the quota API (e.g., Cosmos DB)
Requires Reader or Quota Request Operator permissions on the subscription
Quota API does not replace service-specific limit documentation

use_cases.title: Deployment planning
use_cases.description: Validate available quota capacity before deploying resources to prevent quota exceeded errors during provisioning.
use_cases.target_user: Cloud architects and DevOps engineers planning Azure deployments

use_cases.title: Region selection
use_cases.description: Compare quota availability across candidate regions to find optimal deployment locations with sufficient capacity.
use_cases.target_user: Infrastructure teams evaluating multi-region deployment strategies

use_cases.title: Quota troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Diagnose quota exceeded errors by checking current usage versus limits and requesting increases when needed.
use_cases.target_user: Site reliability engineers resolving deployment failures

prompt_templates.title: Basic quota check
prompt_templates.prompt: Check the current quota and usage for standardDSv3Family VMs in the eastus region for my subscription.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick quota lookup for a specific VM family in one region

prompt_templates.title: Multi-region comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare available quota for standardDSv3Family VMs across eastus, eastus2, westus2, and centralus regions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Finding the best region based on available capacity

prompt_templates.title: Quota increase request
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to increase my standardFSv2Family quota from 50 to 100 vCPUs in eastus. Show me the command to request this increase.
prompt_templates.scenario: Requesting a quota increase via Azure CLI

prompt_templates.title: Full capacity planning
prompt_templates.prompt: List all quotas for Microsoft.Compute in eastus, then show me the usage and available capacity for VM quotas. I plan to deploy 20 more DSv3 VMs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comprehensive capacity validation before a deployment

output_examples.input: Check quota for standardDSv3Family in eastus
output_examples.output: Limit: 350 vCPUs | Current Usage: 50 vCPUs | Available Capacity: 300 vCPUs

output_examples.input: Compare DSv3 quota across eastus, eastus2, westus2
output_examples.output:
Region Comparison:
- eastus: 300 available (350 limit - 50 used)
- eastus2: 180 available (200 limit - 20 used)
- westus2: 450 available (500 limit - 50 used)

Recommendation: westus2 has the highest available capacity.

best_practices: Always run az quota list first to discover the correct quota resource names for your provider
Check quotas in multiple regions before committing to a deployment location
Request quota increases with a 20 percent buffer above immediate needs to accommodate growth
anti_patterns: Do not rely on REST API or Portal as first approach - they show misleading No Limit values
Do not assume No Limit means unlimited capacity - it usually means the quota API does not support that resource
Do not skip quota validation before large deployments - always verify available capacity first

faq.question: What is the azure-quotas skill for?
faq.answer: This skill helps you check Azure service quotas, validate available capacity, compare regions, and request quota increases using Azure CLI commands.

faq.question: Do I need to install anything to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, you need Azure CLI with the quota extension installed. Run: az extension add --name quota

faq.question: Why does the REST API show No Limit for my resource?
faq.answer: No Limit does not mean unlimited capacity. It typically means the quota API does not support that resource provider. Use Azure CLI commands instead, or check the Azure service limits documentation.

faq.question: Which Azure resource providers support quota commands?
faq.answer: Microsoft.Compute, Microsoft.Network, Microsoft.App, Microsoft.Storage, and Microsoft.MachineLearningServices are confirmed working. Microsoft.DocumentDB (Cosmos DB) is not supported.

faq.question: How long does a quota increase request take?
faq.answer: Most adjustable quotas are auto-approved within minutes. Some requests require manual review and may take hours to days. Non-adjustable quotas require an Azure Support ticket.

faq.question: What permissions do I need to check quotas?
faq.answer: Reader permission is sufficient to view quotas. To request quota increases, you need the Quota Request Operator role or higher.
